#!/bin/bash
#
# Remediation script: Ensure auditing of removexattr syscall (both 32-bit
# and 64-bit) for regular users (UID >= 1000) and root, tagged with the
# perm_mod key, and persisted across reboots for both the audit rules.d
# (augenrules) approach and the direct auditctl rules file approach.

set -euo pipefail

readonly SYSCALL="removexattr"
readonly KEY="perm_mod"
readonly AUID_MIN=1000
readonly AUID_MAX=4294967295

# Files that may be used depending on the rule-loading approach
readonly RULES_D_DIR="/etc/audit/rules.d"
readonly RULES_D_FILE="${RULES_D_DIR}/${SYSCALL}.rules"
readonly AUDIT_RULES_FILE="/etc/audit/audit.rules"

# Build the four required rule lines (b32/b64 x auid>=1000 / auid=0)
build_rules() {
	local arch="$1"
	cat <<EOF
-a always,exit -F arch=${arch} -S ${SYSCALL} -F auid>=${AUID_MIN} -F auid!=${AUID_MAX} -F key=${KEY}
-a always,exit -F arch=${arch} -S ${SYSCALL} -F auid=0 -F key=${KEY}
EOF
}

# Check whether a given rule line already exists (exact match ignoring
# leading/trailing whitespace) in a file.
rule_exists_in_file() {
	local rule="$1"
	local file="$2"
	[[ -f "${file}" ]] || return 1
	grep -Fxq -- "${rule}" "${file}"
}

# Append rule to file if not present
ensure_rule_in_file() {
	local rule="$1"
	local file="$2"

	if ! rule_exists_in_file "${rule}" "${file}"; then
		echo "${rule}" >>"${file}"
	fi
}

main() {
	if [[ "$(id -u)" -ne 0 ]]; then
		echo "This script must be run as root." >&2
		exit 1
	fi

	# Determine architecture(s) to cover. On a 64-bit system we need both
	# b32 and b64 rules.
	local arches=("b64" "b32")

	# Collect all required rule lines
	local required_rules=()
	local arch
	for arch in "${arches[@]}"; do
		while IFS= read -r line; do
			[[ -n "${line}" ]] && required_rules+=("${line}")
		done < <(build_rules "${arch}")
	done

	# ------------------------------------------------------------------
	# 1. Ensure rules.d approach (augenrules) has the rules
	# ------------------------------------------------------------------
	mkdir -p "${RULES_D_DIR}"

	if [[ ! -f "${RULES_D_FILE}" ]]; then
		touch "${RULES_D_FILE}"
		chmod 0640 "${RULES_D_FILE}"
	fi

	local rule
	for rule in "${required_rules[@]}"; do
		ensure_rule_in_file "${rule}" "${RULES_D_FILE}"
	done

	# ------------------------------------------------------------------
	# 2. Ensure direct rule-loading approach (audit.rules) has the rules,
	#    if that file exists (some systems generate it solely via
	#    augenrules, in which case we still keep it in sync defensively).
	# ------------------------------------------------------------------
	if [[ -f "${AUDIT_RULES_FILE}" ]]; then
		for rule in "${required_rules[@]}"; do
			ensure_rule_in_file "${rule}" "${AUDIT_RULES_FILE}"
		done
	fi

	# ------------------------------------------------------------------
	# 3. Regenerate rules via augenrules if available, to merge rules.d
	#    into audit.rules and load them, ensuring persistence across
	#    reboots regardless of which mechanism is authoritative.
	# ------------------------------------------------------------------
	if command -v augenrules >/dev/null 2>&1; then
		augenrules --load || true
	fi

	# ------------------------------------------------------------------
	# 4. Load the rules immediately into the running kernel audit system
	#    using auditctl, so the change takes effect without a reboot.
	# ------------------------------------------------------------------
	if command -v auditctl >/dev/null 2>&1; then
		for rule in "${required_rules[@]}"; do
			# shellcheck disable=SC2086
			auditctl ${rule} >/dev/null 2>&1 || true
		done
	fi

	echo "Audit rules for ${SYSCALL} (key=${KEY}) have been ensured."
}

main "$@"

#!/bin/bash
#
# Remediation script: Disable kernel image loading via kexec (kexec_load)
#
# This script sets kernel.kexec_load_disabled=1 both at runtime and
# persistently via a sysctl configuration file. This prevents loading
# a replacement kernel image into memory (kexec), which could otherwise
# be used by a privileged attacker to boot into a malicious kernel
# without triggering a normal system reboot.
#
# NOTE: Once disabled at runtime, this setting cannot be re-enabled
# without rebooting the system (the sysctl is write-once to 1).
#
# This script is idempotent: it can be run multiple times safely.

set -euo pipefail

readonly SYSCTL_PARAM="kernel.kexec_load_disabled"
readonly DESIRED_VALUE="1"
readonly PERSIST_FILE="/etc/sysctl.d/60-kexec_load_disabled.conf"

# --- Ensure persistent configuration is present ---
if [ -f "${PERSIST_FILE}" ]; then
    if grep -Eq "^\s*${SYSCTL_PARAM}\s*=\s*${DESIRED_VALUE}\s*$" "${PERSIST_FILE}"; then
        : # Already correctly configured, nothing to do
    else
        # Remove any existing (possibly incorrect) entries for this key, then add correct one
        sed -i "/^\s*${SYSCTL_PARAM}\s*=/d" "${PERSIST_FILE}"
        echo "${SYSCTL_PARAM} = ${DESIRED_VALUE}" >> "${PERSIST_FILE}"
    fi
else
    printf '%s = %s\n' "${SYSCTL_PARAM}" "${DESIRED_VALUE}" > "${PERSIST_FILE}"
fi

chmod 0644 "${PERSIST_FILE}"

# --- Also ensure no other sysctl config file overrides this setting ---
# Search other sysctl config locations for conflicting settings and fix them,
# excluding our own managed file.
mapfile -t OTHER_FILES < <(grep -rlE "^\s*${SYSCTL_PARAM}\s*=" /etc/sysctl.conf /etc/sysctl.d/*.conf /run/sysctl.d/*.conf /usr/lib/sysctl.d/*.conf 2>/dev/null | grep -v -F "${PERSIST_FILE}" || true)

if [ "${#OTHER_FILES[@]}" -gt 0 ]; then
    for f in "${OTHER_FILES[@]}"; do
        if [ -f "$f" ]; then
            sed -i "/^\s*${SYSCTL_PARAM}\s*=/d" "$f"
        fi
    done
fi

# --- Apply setting at runtime ---

```bash
#!/bin/bash
#
# Remediation script: Disable kernel image loading via kexec (kexec_load)
#
# This script sets kernel.kexec_load_disabled=1 both at runtime and
# persistently via a sysctl configuration file. This prevents loading
# a replacement kernel image into memory (kexec), which could otherwise
# be used by a privileged attacker to boot into a malicious kernel
# without triggering a normal system reboot.
#
# NOTE: Once disabled at runtime, this setting cannot be re-enabled
# without rebooting the system (the sysctl is write-once to 1).
#
# This script is idempotent: it can be run multiple times safely.

set -euo pipefail

readonly SYSCTL_PARAM="kernel.kexec_load_disabled"
readonly DESIRED_VALUE="1"
readonly PERSIST_FILE="/etc/sysctl.d/60-kexec_load_disabled.conf"

# --- Ensure persistent configuration is present ---
if [ -f "${PERSIST_FILE}" ]; then
    if grep -Eq "^\s*${SYSCTL_PARAM}\s*=\s*${DESIRED_VALUE}\s*$" "${PERSIST_FILE}"; then
        : # Already correctly configured, nothing to do
    else
        # Remove any existing (possibly incorrect) entries for this key, then add correct one
        sed -i "/^\s*${SYSCTL_PARAM}\s*=/d" "${PERSIST_FILE}"
        echo "${SYSCTL_PARAM} = ${DESIRED_VALUE}" >> "${PERSIST_FILE}"
    fi
else
    printf '%s = %s\n' "${SYSCTL_PARAM}" "${DESIRED_VALUE}" > "${PERSIST_FILE}"
fi

chmod 0644 "${PERSIST_FILE}"

# --- Also ensure no other sysctl config file overrides this setting ---
# Search other sysctl config locations for conflicting settings and fix them,
# excluding our own managed file.
mapfile -t OTHER_FILES < <(grep -rlE "^\s*${SYSCTL_PARAM}\s*=" /etc/sysctl.conf /etc/sysctl.d/*.conf /run/sysctl.d/*.conf /usr/lib/sysctl.d/*.conf 2>/dev/null | grep -v -F "${PERSIST_FILE}" || true)

if [ "${#OTHER_FILES[@]}" -gt 0 ]; then
    for f in "${OTHER_FILES[@]}"; do
        if [ -f "$f" ]; then
            sed -i "/^\s*${SYSCTL_PARAM}\s*=/d" "$f"
        fi
    done
fi

# --- Apply setting at runtime ---
current_value="$(sysctl -n "${SYSCTL_PARAM}" 2>/dev/null || echo "")"

if [ "${current_value}" != "${DESIRED_VALUE}" ]; then
    sysctl -w "${SYSCTL_PARAM}=${DESIRED_VALUE}" >/dev/null
fi

# --- Reload sysctl settings to ensure consistency (best effort) ---
sysctl --system >/dev/null 2>&1 || true

exit 0
```

#!/bin/bash
#
# Remediation script: Ensure OpenSSH server MACs are governed by the
# system-wide crypto policy (RHEL 8) rather than a local override.
#
# This script:
#   1. Removes any explicit "MACs" directive from sshd_config and any
#      files included via Include (typically /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/*.conf),
#      since a local MACs override would take precedence over / diverge
#      from the system-wide crypto policy.
#   2. Ensures the system-wide crypto policy is applied to sshd via the
#      standard include mechanism (Include line referencing the
#      crypto-policies generated snippet), which is idempotent.
#   3. Validates sshd configuration syntax before declaring success.
#
# The script is idempotent: running it multiple times results in the
# same final state without errors.

set -euo pipefail

SSHD_CONFIG="/etc/ssh/sshd_config"
CRYPTO_POLICY_SSHD_INCLUDE="/etc/crypto-policies/back-ends/opensshserver.config"
SSHD_CONFIG_DIR="/etc/ssh/sshd_config.d"

if [[ ! -f "${SSHD_CONFIG}" ]]; then
    echo "Error: ${SSHD_CONFIG} not found." >&2
    exit 1
fi

# Ensure the crypto-policies back-end file for OpenSSH server exists.
# It is normally generated by the crypto-policies package/update-crypto-policies.
if [[ ! -e "${CRYPTO_POLICY_SSHD_INCLUDE}" ]]; then
    if command -v update-crypto-policies >/dev/null 2>&1; then
        update-crypto-policies --set "$(update-crypto-policies --show 2>/dev/null || echo DEFAULT)" >/dev/null 2>&1 || true
    fi
fi

if [[ ! -e "${CRYPTO_POLICY_SSHD_INCLUDE}" ]]; then
    echo "Error: crypto-policies back-end file ${CRYPTO_POLICY_SSHD_INCLUDE} is missing." >&2
    echo "Ensure the crypto-policies package is installed and update-crypto-policies has been run." >&2
    exit 1
fi

remove_macs_override() {
    local file="$1"

    if [[ ! -f "${file}" ]]; then
        return 0
    fi

    if grep -Eiq '^[[:space:]]*MACs[[:space:]]+' "${file}"; then
        local tmpfile
        tmpfile="$(mktemp)"
        # Remove any line that sets MACs (case-insensitive directive match)
        grep -Eiv '^[[:space:]]*MACs[[:space:]]+' "${file}" > "${tmpfile}"

        # Preserve ownership and permissions of the original file
        local orig_owner orig_perms
        orig_owner="$(stat -c '%u:%g' "${file}")"
        orig_perms="$(stat -c '%a' "${file}")"

        cp "${tmpfile}" "${file}"
        rm -f "${tmpfile}"

        chown "${orig_owner}" "${file}"
        chmod "${orig_perms}" "${file}"

        echo "Removed local MACs override from ${file}."
    fi
}

# Remove MACs override from the main sshd_config
remove_macs_override "${SSHD_CONFIG}"

# Remove MACs override from any drop-in files included by sshd_config
if [[ -d "${SSHD_CONFIG_DIR}" ]]; then
    while IFS= read -r -d '' conf_file; do
        remove_macs_override "${conf_file}"
    done < <(find "${SSHD_CONFIG_DIR}" -maxdepth 1 -type f -name '*.conf' -print0 2>/dev/null)
fi

# Ensure sshd_config includes the crypto-policies drop-in directory,
# so the system-wide policy (which sets MACs) is honored.
# RHEL 8's default sshd_config already contains:
#   Include /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/*.conf
# We verify this include line is present and active (not commented out).
if ! grep -Eq '^[[:space:]]*Include[[:space:]]+/etc/ssh/sshd_config\.d/\*\.conf[[:space:]]*$' "${SSHD_CONFIG}"; then
    # Insert the Include directive at the top of the file if missing,
    # since sshd uses first-match-wins semantics for most keywords.
    tmpfile="$(mktemp)"
    {
        echo "Include /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/*.conf"
        cat "${SSHD_CONFIG}"
    } > "${tmpfile}"

    orig_owner="$(stat -c '%u:%g' "${SSHD_CONFIG}")"
    orig_perms="$(stat -c '%a' "${SSHD_CONFIG}")"

    cp "${tmpfile}" "${SSHD_CONFIG}"
    rm -f "${tmpfile}"

    chown "${orig_owner}" "${SSHD_CONFIG}"
    chmod "${orig_perms}" "${SSHD_CONFIG}"

    echo "Added missing Include directive for sshd_config.d to ${SSHD_CONFIG}."
fi

# Validate the resulting sshd configuration
if command -v sshd >/dev/null 2>&1; then
    if ! sshd -t 2>/dev/null; then
        echo "Error: sshd configuration test failed after remediation." >&2
        sshd -t
        exit 1
    fi
fi

echo "SSH server MAC algorithms are now governed by the system-wide crypto policy."

exit 0

#!/bin/bash
#
# Remediation script: Ensure auditd logs execution of /usr/sbin/usermod
# by non-system users (auid >= 1000) as a privileged executable-access event.
#
# This script is idempotent and configures both:
#   - /etc/audit/rules.d/*.rules (used by augenrules, persistent across reboot)
#   - /etc/audit/audit.rules (used by auditctl -R directly, if that mechanism is in use)
#
# The rule watches /usr/sbin/usermod for execute access (perm=x), excludes
# system accounts (auid>=1000, auid!=unset/4294967295), and tags it with
# key=privileged.

set -euo pipefail

AUDIT_BIN="/usr/sbin/usermod"

if [ ! -e "${AUDIT_BIN}" ]; then
    echo "ERROR: ${AUDIT_BIN} not found on this system." >&2
    exit 1
fi

# Determine architecture-specific rule lines. RHEL8 uses -F arch= for exec-based watches
# via -a always,exit -F path=... but for a simple execute-access watch on a specific file,
# we use -w (file watch) with -p x permission, which does not require arch qualifiers.
RULE_LINE="-w ${AUDIT_BIN} -p x -k privileged"

# --- Function: ensure rule present in a given rules file, replacing any stale
#     rule referencing the same path but with different perms/key, and adding
#     if not already present verbatim.
ensure_rule_in_file() {
    local file="$1"
    local rule="$2"

    # Create file if it doesn't exist
    if [ ! -f "${file}" ]; then
        touch "${file}"
    fi

    # Remove any existing rule lines referencing this binary path (any perms/key variant)
    # to avoid duplicate/conflicting watches, then append the canonical rule.
    if grep -Eq -- "^-w[[:space:]]+${AUDIT_BIN//\//\\/}([[:space:]]|\$)" "${file}"; then
        # Check if the exact canonical rule already exists
        if grep -Fxq -- "${rule}" "${file}"; then
            : # already correct, nothing to do
        else
            # Remove stale variants of the watch on this path
            sed -i -E "\|^-w[[:space:]]+${AUDIT_BIN//\//\\/}([[:space:]]|\$)|d" "${file}"
            echo "${rule}" >> "${file}"
        fi
    else
        # No existing watch on this path; add it
        echo "${rule}" >> "${file}"
    fi
}

# --- Determine which persistent rules directory/file to use ---
RULES_D_DIR="/etc/audit/rules.d"
RULES_D_FILE="${RULES_D_DIR}/privileged-usermod.rules"
AUDIT_RULES_FILE="/etc/audit/audit.rules"

mkdir -p "${RULES_D_DIR}"

# Always ensure the rules.d file (used by augenrules) has the rule.
ensure_rule_in_file "${RULES_D_FILE}" "${RULE_LINE}"

# Also ensure the flat audit.rules file has the rule, in case the system
# is using auditctl -R /etc/audit/audit.rules directly rather than augenrules.
if [ -f "${AUDIT_RULES_FILE}" ]; then
    ensure_rule_in_file "${AUDIT_RULES_FILE}" "${RULE_LINE}"
fi

# --- Regenerate consolidated audit.rules from rules.d if augenrules is available ---
if command -v augenrules >/dev/null 2>&1; then
    augenrules --load || true
elif command -v auditctl >/dev/null 2>&1; then
    # Fallback: load rules directly via auditctl if augenrules is not present
    auditctl -R "${AUDIT_RULES_FILE}" || true
fi

# --- Load the rule into the live kernel audit ruleset immediately, if not already present ---
if command -v auditctl >/dev/null 2>&1; then
    if ! auditctl -l 2>/dev/null | grep -Fq -- "${AUDIT_BIN}" ; then
        auditctl -w "${AUDIT_BIN}" -p x -k privileged || true
    fi
fi

exit 0

# Ensure AIDE is installed
if ! rpm -q aide &>/dev/null; then
    dnf install -y aide

AIDE_DB_DIR="/var/lib/aide"
AIDE_DB="${AIDE_DB_DIR}/aide.db.gz"
AIDE_DB_NEW="${AIDE_DB_DIR}/aide.db.new.gz"
AIDE_CONF="/etc/aide.conf"

# Idempotency check: only initialize if the active database does not already exist
if [ ! -f "${AIDE_DB}" ]; then
    echo "No existing AIDE database found at ${AIDE_DB}. Initializing baseline..."

        AIDE_BIN="aide"
    else
        echo "Error: aide binary not found after installation." >&2
        exit 1
    fi

    # Run initialization
    if [ -f "${AIDE_CONF}" ]; then
        "${AIDE_BIN}" --config="${AIDE_CONF}" --init
    else
        "${AIDE_BIN}" --init
    fi

    # Some builds produce aide.db.new.gz, others produce aide.db.gz directly.
    if [ -f "${AIDE_DB_NEW}" ]; then
        mv -f "${AIDE_DB_NEW}" "${AIDE_DB}"
    elif [ -f "${AIDE_DB}" ]; then
        : # already in place
    else
        echo "Error: AIDE initialization did not produce a database file." >&2
        exit 1
    fi

    echo "AIDE baseline database installed at ${AIDE_DB}."
else
    echo "AIDE database already exists at ${AIDE_DB}. No action needed."
fi

exit 0

SYSCALL="setxattr"

declare -a RULES=(
    "-a always,exit -F arch=b32 -S ${SYSCALL} -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=unset -k ${KEY}"
    "-a always,exit -F arch=b32 -S ${SYSCALL} -F auid=0 -k ${KEY}"
    "-a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S ${SYSCALL} -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=unset -k ${KEY}"
    "-a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S ${SYSCALL} -F auid=0 -k ${KEY}"
)

# Ensure rules directory exists
mkdir -p /etc/audit/rules.d

# Create the rules file if it does not exist
if [[ ! -f "${RULES_FILE}" ]]; then
    touch "${RULES_FILE}"
fi

# Add each rule if it is not already present (idempotent)
for rule in "${RULES[@]}"; do
    if ! grep -Fxq -- "${rule}" "${RULES_FILE}" 2>/dev/null; then
        echo "${rule}" >> "${RULES_FILE}"
    fi
done

# Also ensure the rules are present in the currently active auditd.rules
# (in case augenrules has not been used yet, or auditctl-based loading is used)
ACTIVE_RULES_FILE="/etc/audit/audit.rules"

# Determine whether the system uses augenrules or auditctl for loading rules
USE_AUGENRULES=0
if command -v augenrules >/dev/null 2>&1; then
    USE_AUGENRULES=1
fi

# Reload the rules using the appropriate mechanism
if [[ "${USE_AUGENRULES}" -eq 1 ]]; then
    augenrules --load
else
    # Fall back to auditctl-based persistence: ensure rules are also
    # reflected in /etc/audit/audit.rules for systems without augenrules
    if [[ -f "${ACTIVE_RULES_FILE}" ]]; then
        for rule in "${RULES[@]}"; do
            if ! grep -Fxq -- "${rule}" "${ACTIVE_RULES_FILE}" 2>/dev/null; then
                echo "${rule}" >> "${ACTIVE_RULES_FILE}"
            fi
        done
    fi
    auditctl -R "${RULES_FILE}" || true
fi

# Verify auditd service is enabled and restart to apply changes safely
if systemctl is-enabled auditd >/dev/null 2>&1; then
    systemctl restart auditd || true
fi

exit 0

# Add each rule if it is not already present (idempotent)
for rule in "${RULES[@]}"; do
    if ! grep -Fxq -- "${rule}" "${RULES_FILE}" 2>/dev/null; then
        echo "${rule}" >> "${RULES_FILE}"
    fi
done

# Also ensure the rules are present in the currently active auditd.rules
# (in case augenrules has not been used yet, or auditctl-based loading is used)
ACTIVE_RULES_FILE="/etc/audit/audit.rules"

# Determine whether the system uses augenrules or auditctl for loading rules
USE_AUGENRULES=0
if command -v augenrules >/dev/null 2>&1; then
    USE_AUGENRULES=1
fi

# Reload the rules using the appropriate mechanism
if [[ "${USE_AUGENRULES}" -eq 1 ]]; then
    augenrules --load
else
    # Fall back to auditctl-based persistence: ensure rules are also
    # reflected in /etc/audit/audit.rules for systems without augenrules
    if [[ -f "${ACTIVE_RULES_FILE}" ]]; then
        for rule in "${RULES[@]}"; do
            if ! grep -Fxq -- "${rule}" "${ACTIVE_RULES_FILE}" 2>/dev/null; then
                echo "${rule}" >> "${ACTIVE_RULES_FILE}"
            fi
        done
    fi
    auditctl -R "${RULES_FILE}" || true
fi

# Verify auditd service is enabled and restart to apply changes safely
if systemctl is-enabled auditd >/dev/null 2>&1; then
    systemctl restart auditd || true
fi

exit 0
